results.question:
A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 30 minutes after he had loss of consciousness. His wife 
says he was making dinner when he fell on the kitchen floor. He has hypertension and his only medication is lisinopril. He has no history of operative procedures. En route, his temperature was 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse was 90/min, respirations were 10/min and labored, and blood pressure was 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 40% oxygen by face mask showed an oxygen saturation 
of 99%. On arrival, he is unresponsive. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 90/min, ventilatory rate is 10/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an F
IO2 of 0.4 shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. On examination, Doll's eye (oculocephalic) maneuver shows absent eye 
movements, and the corneal reflex is absent. On ice-water caloric testing, there is no nystagmus or deviation of the eyes toward the ear being irrigated. CT scan of the head shows a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. When the patient's wife is notified of the patient's condition, she says he wanted to be an organ donor but does not have an advance directive. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Obtain guidance from the institutional ethics board
options.B: Contact the organ procurement organization to assess potential for organ donation
options.C: Check for potential organ recipients currently listed at this facility
options.D: Inform the family that organ donation cannot proceed due to the absence of documented advance care planning
options.E: Inform the family that organ donation is not possible due to the patient's chronic hypertension

results.correct_answer: Contact the organ procurement organization to assess potential for organ donation
results.predicted_answer: Contact the organ procurement organization to assess potential for organ donation
results.response:
The patient has suffered a catastrophic neurological event (ruptured cerebral aneurysm) and shows clinical signs consistent with brain death: unresponsive with GCS 3, absent brainstem reflexes (absent oculocephalic reflex, absent corneal reflex, and absent vestibulo-ocular reflex on caloric testing). The CT scan confirms the cause. The wife mentions the patient wanted to be an organ donor, but there is no advance directive.

In the United States, organ donation can proceed after brain death is confirmed, even without a written advance directive, if the next of kin (in this case, the wife) consents. The absence of an advance directive does not preclude donation; instead, the family can authorize it based on the patient's wishes. The chronic hypertension is not an absolute contraindication for organ donation; each organ is evaluated individually for suitability.

The next step is to contact the organ procurement organization (OPO) to assess the potential for organ donation. The OPO will help manage the process, including confirming brain death, discussing with the family, and evaluating organs. This is standard protocol per US guidelines.

Option A (ethics board) is not necessary as this is a straightforward scenario with expressed wish by the patient and no ethical conflict. Option C (checking for recipients) is not appropriate; the OPO handles allocation. Option D is incorrect because absence of advance directive does not prevent donation if family consents. Option E is incorrect as hypertension is not an absolute bar to donation.

Thus, the most appropriate step is to contact the OPO.
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
A 36-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 39 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. She reports painful contractions 
that occur every 2 to 3 minutes and last 60 seconds. She has not had vaginal bleeding. She has no history of serious illness, and 
pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/70 
mm Hg. The abdomen is nontender and consistent in size with a 39-week gestation. External fetal monitoring shows a heart rate 
of 150/min with moderate variability, several spontaneous accelerations, and no decelerations. An external tocometer shows 
regular uterine contractions every 2 to 3 minutes. The cervix is 6 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at 0 station. 
Ultrasonography shows the fetus in a cephalic presentation. The membranes are artificially ruptured, yielding copious clear fluid. 
Thirty minutes later, the cervix is 9 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at +1 station. A fetal heart tracing is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Instruct the patient to initiate maternal expulsive efforts
options.B: Infusion of warmed saline into the amniotic cavity

results.predicted_answer: Infusion of warmed saline into the amniotic cavity
results.response:
The fetal heart tracing shows variable decelerations, which are common during the second stage of labor and often due to cord compression. Given that the cervix is 9 cm dilated (almost fully dilated) and the vertex is at +1 station, the patient is in the late active phase of labor. Variable decelerations can be managed with conservative measures such as changing maternal position (e.g., lateral decubitus), administering oxygen, or amnioinfusion. However, in this case, the membranes have been artificially ruptured, and copious clear fluid was noted, but variable decelerations have appeared. Amnioinfusion (option B) is a recognized technique to relieve cord compression by infusing warmed saline into the amniotic cavity to cushion the cord and reduce decelerations. This is particularly appropriate when there are recurrent variable decelerations, as it can help avoid more invasive interventions if successful.

Option A (instruct expulsive efforts) is premature since the cervix is not fully dilated (9 cm, not 10 cm). Option C (continue monitoring) might be acceptable if the decelerations were mild and intermittent, but given the context, proactive management is preferred to prevent worsening. Option D (vacuum extraction) is not indicated as the cervix is not fully dilated, and the station is only +1, which is not low enough for assisted delivery. Option E (emergent cesarean) is excessive at this point, as there is no evidence of fetal acidosis (baseline heart rate and variability are reassuring), and variable decelerations can often be managed with amnioinfusion.

Thus, the most appropriate next step is infusion of warmed saline into the amniotic cavity (amnioinfusion) to alleviate the variable decelerations.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 27-year-old w oman comes to the office because of a 3-week history of nasal congestion, cough productive of sputum, 
intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate pain over her cheeks. She has had similar symptoms three to four times yearly during the past 5 years. The symptoms last 2 to 8 weeks and generally resolve spontaneously. She has had two episodes of bacterial pneumonia during the past 5 years; both episodes resolved with antibiotic therapy. Her only current 
medication is acetaminophen as needed for headache. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum study results show decreased IgA, IgG, and IgM concentrations. Antibiotic therapy is begun, and the patient’s condition improves. Which of the following is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection in this patient?

options.D: Administration of an intranasal influenza vaccine

results.response:
The patient has low IgA, IgG, and IgM — indicating a primary immunodeficiency (likely common variable immunodeficiency or a similar antibody deficiency).  
Recurrent sinopulmonary infections are typical, and IVIG replacement is the standard therapy to prevent recurrent infections in patients with significant antibody deficiency.  

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 37-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-year history of mild to moderate fatigue. The fatigue waxes and wanes 
without relation to activity. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He drinks three glasses of whiskey 
weekly. He has had two lifetime female sexual partners; he uses condoms consistently. He immigrated to the United States from 
China 15 years ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies 
are shown: 
 
Serum    
 
  
  
 
 
 
  Creatinine  1.2 mg/dL  
ALT  85 U/L  
AST  60 U/L  
HBsAg  positive  
Anti-HCV  negative  
Anti-HBs negative  
IgG anti -HBc positive  
Blood    
Hematocrit  35% 
Hemoglobin  12 g/dL  
MCV  82 µm3 
 
Without treatment of his current condition, this patient is most likely to develop which of the following?

options.B: Cryoglobulinemia of the mixed essential type
options.E: Polyarteritis nodosa as a future complication

results.response:
The patient has chronic hepatitis B infection (HBsAg positive, IgG anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs negative).  
Given his mildly elevated ALT/AST and no signs of cirrhosis currently, the most likely long-term complication without treatment is progression to hepatocellular carcinoma (hepatoma).  
Chronic hepatitis B is a major risk factor for hepatoma even without cirrhosis, especially in patients from endemic regions like China.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 10-year-old boy is brought to the office because of moderate right knee pain. He is in no distress. Medical history is remarkable 
for hemophilia A. Examination of the right knee shows warmth and swelling; there is pain with flexion. Range of motion is limited by pain and swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy for this patient?

options.E: Plasma that has been frozen and contains clotting factors

results.response:
The patient has hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) and presents with signs of a hemarthrosis (warmth, swelling, pain, limited range of motion). The most appropriate therapy is to replace the deficient clotting factor.

- **A. Desmopressin (DDAVP)** is used in mild hemophilia A to stimulate release of stored factor VIII, but it is not sufficient for moderate bleeding like a hemarthrosis.
- **B. Recombinant factor VIII replacement** is the standard, specific treatment for acute bleeding in hemophilia A.
- **C. Factor IX replacement therapy** is for hemophilia B, not hemophilia A.
- **D. Iron supplementation** is not indicated for acute bleeding management in hemophilia.
- **E. Fresh frozen plasma** contains factor VIII but in low concentrations and carries infection risks; it is not first-line when safer, concentrated factor products are available.

Therefore, recombinant factor VIII is the most appropriate therapy.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-week history of visual changes in his right eye. During this period, he has 
noticed that the lines of his daily crossword puzzle look curved, and the blinds in his apartment appear wavy. He wears magnifying 
lenses for reading. He has not had pain in his eye or photophobia. He has no history of trauma to the area. On examination, visual 
acuity is 20/200 in the right eye and 20/100 in the left eye. Both pupils are round and reactive to light. Palpation of both globes 
through closed eyelids shows no abnormalities. There is normal red reflex bilaterally. The lenses appear clear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 28-year-ol d man comes to the clinic because of a 2-day history of a red, severely painful lesion on his left forearm. He says that 
he felt stinging pain as he put his shirt on in the dark 2 days ago; the pain was so severe he immediately threw down the shirt and 
saw a spider running across the floor. He returned yesterday from a 1-week trip to the mountains in the southeastern United 
States. He has type 1 diabetes mellitus treated with lisinopril and insulin. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. He is sexually 
active with two male partners and uses condoms inconsistently. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the lesion 
is shown. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following physical findings is most likely to appear during the next 24 hours?

options.A: Central eschar formation at the site of the lesion
options.B: Necrosis of the distal portion of the left limb
options.D: Palpable purpura distributed over the trunk and upper extremities
options.E: Progression of erythema with advancing borders adjacent to the lesion
options.F: Erythematous, tender linear extensions progressing toward the epitrochlear lymph nodes

results.correct_answer: Central eschar formation at the site of the lesion
results.predicted_answer: Central eschar formation at the site of the lesion
results.response:
The patient’s story — sudden severe pain after a possible spider bite while dressing, recent travel to the southeastern US — is classic for a brown recluse spider bite (Loxosceles reclusa).  
Brown recluse bites often progress over 24–72 hours from initial redness and pain to central blistering or necrosis with a characteristic **central eschar (necrotic ulcer)** surrounded by erythema.  
Other options:  
- **B (distal limb necrosis)** is too extreme this early.  
- **C (diffuse swelling)** can occur but is less specific than eschar.  
- **D (palpable purpura)** suggests vasculitis, not fitting the bite progression.  
- **E (advancing borders)** is more like cellulitis/erysipelas.  
- **F (lymphangitic streaks)** suggests bacterial infection, not brown recluse.  

Most likely next 24h finding is **central eschar formation**.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 37-year-old man comes to the clinic for a health maintenance examination prior to employment. He feels well. He has no history 
of serious illness and takes no medications. One year ago, his blood pressure was 136/85 mm Hg. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 100 kg (220 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m
2. Today, his blood pressure is 138/87 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. 
In addition to recommending weight loss, which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent cardiovascular morbidity in this patient?

options.A: Initiation of an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor
options.C: Initiate a dietary approach to stop hypertension

results.correct_answer: Initiate a dietary approach to stop hypertension

is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 118/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no 
abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:

results.question:
Two days after admission to the hospital for treatment of a fractured femur and closed head injury sustained in a motor vehicle 
collision, an 8-year-old girl has a brief, generalized tonic-clonic seizure. CT scan of the head on admission disclosed no abnormalities. She has no history of serious illness. Oral acetaminophen was initiated for pain, and the patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation of the fracture. She receives no routine medications. Today, she appears drowsy but responds to 
questions appropriately. She is at the 55th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 106/64 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation 
of 95%. Pupils are equal and reactive to light and accommodation. Ocular movements are full. Muscle strength is 5/5, and deep tendon reflexes are normal. Results of serum studies are shown: 
 
Na+ 122 mEq/L  
K+ 3.8 mEq/L  
Cl− 94 mEq/L  
HCO 3− 24 mEq/L  
Glucose  80 mg/dL  
Calcium  8.8 mg/dL  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Intravenous infusion of a 3% sodium chloride solution
options.C: Administer a loading dose of fosphenytoin
options.D: Begin therapy with isotonic saline at 150% of the standard maintenance fluid rate.
options.F: Obtain magnetic resonance imaging of the brain

results.correct_answer: Intravenous infusion of a 3% sodium chloride solution
results.predicted_answer: Intravenous infusion of a 3% sodium chloride solution
results.response:
The patient has hyponatremia (Na⁺ 122 mEq/L) two days after a closed head injury and femur fracture, with a new seizure and drowsiness.  
Given the acute drop in sodium and neurological symptoms, the most urgent step is to correct the sodium to prevent cerebral edema and further seizures.  
The appropriate acute management for symptomatic hyponatremia is **3% hypertonic saline** to raise serum sodium by about 4–6 mEq/L in the first few hours.  

Other options:  
- Desmopressin would worsen or be inappropriate here (likely SIADH or cerebral salt wasting post-head injury).  
- Fosphenytoin is for seizure prophylaxis but doesn’t treat the underlying cause.  
- Isotonic saline at 150% maintenance is not the standard acute treatment for symptomatic hyponatremia.  
- EEG and MRI may be needed later but not the most urgent next step.  

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 43-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-week history of fatigue and mild pain in his right armpit. He has not had 
fever or weight loss. Medical history is remarkable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypercholesterolemia. Medications are 
metformin, simvastatin, and aspirin. He is a social worker and frequently visits clients in homeless shelters. He lives alone with 
two dogs and a cat and has one male sexual partner. Vital signs are within normal limits. He appears well. Examination of the 
right upper extremity shows an erythematous axilla with a 4-cm, mobile, tender, nonfluctuant axillary lymph node and a tender, 
1-cm epitrochlear lymph node. Abdominal examination shows no hepatosplenomegaly. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
The patient has a history of contact with cats (owns a cat), presents with tender regional lymphadenopathy (axillary and epitrochlear) without systemic symptoms, and has no signs of abscess formation. This fits well with **cat-scratch disease** caused by *Bartonella henselae*.  

Other options:  
- Castleman disease → usually causes diffuse lymphadenopathy, less likely localized tender nodes.  
- Hidradenitis suppurativa → affects apocrine glands, would present with abscesses/nodules in skin, not isolated mobile lymph nodes.  
- Peripheral T-cell lymphoma → usually nontender, progressive lymphadenopathy.  
- Tuberculosis → possible but less likely without risk factors other than homeless shelter exposure; more common presentation would be pulmonary or systemic symptoms.  

<answer>B</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:

history of serious illness and receives no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities.

results.question:
A surgical intensive care unit observes that the number of days that patients have central venous catheters in place and the number 
of bloodstream infections have been increasing. A multidisciplinary team from the unit would like to decrease the number of days that central venous catheters are in place in order to improve performance in this area. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Survey the unit's clinical staff to determine their perspective on the primary barriers to the timely removal of central venous catheters.
options.B: Routinely replace central venous catheters at 5-day intervals
options.C: Post reminders in prominent locations throughout the unit prompting clinicians to review the necessity of central venous catheters.
options.D: Design a controlled trial to evaluate the impact of including a daily reminder on patient rounding sheets to reassess the necessity of a central venous catheter.

results.correct_answer: Survey the unit's clinical staff to determine their perspective on the primary barriers to the timely removal of central venous catheters.
results.predicted_answer: Post reminders in prominent locations throughout the unit prompting clinicians to review the necessity of central venous catheters.
results.response:

results.question:
A 17-year-old girl comes to the office because of a 3-month history of daily moderate epigastric pain that is more severe after 
meals. Three weeks ago, she had watery diarrhea for 2 days, which resolved spontaneously. She has not had vomiting. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination, including abdominal examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

options.C: Stool analysis for ova and parasites
options.D: Fecal sample sent for bacterial pathogen identification

results.response:
The patient's presentation of postprandial epigastric pain and a transient episode of diarrhea suggests a possible upper gastrointestinal issue, such as peptic ulcer disease or gastritis. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy allows for direct visualization of the mucosa, biopsy for H. pylori, and diagnosis of inflammatory or ulcerative conditions, making it the most definitive test in this scenario.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 23-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of intermittent coughing spasms that began 
suddenly while he was attempting to clean mold from his garage with a mixture of acetic acid and cleaning agents. The cough is 
not productive. He reports shortness of breath only during the coughing spasms. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no 
medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use other substances. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and 
weighs 75 kg (165 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen 
saturation of 94%. The patient has periodic paroxysmal coughing during the examination. Oropharynx is clear. Auscultation of 
the lungs discloses bilateral wheezes throughout all lung fields. There is no use of accessory muscles of respiration. The remainder 
of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Chest x-ray shows mild hyperexpansion of the lungs bilaterally. Results 
of arterial blood gas analysis on room air are shown: 
 
PO2 72 mm Hg  
PCO2 38 mm Hg  
pH 7.41 
HCO 3− 23 mEq/L  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?
results.response:
The patient presents with acute onset of coughing spasms, shortness of breath during spasms, bilateral wheezes, mild hypoxemia (PO2 72 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 94%), normal PCO2 (38 mm Hg), normal pH (7.41), and normal bicarbonate (23 mEq/L). The chest x-ray shows mild hyperexpansion, which is consistent with air trapping. The history suggests exposure to irritants (acetic acid and cleaning agents) which may have caused bronchospasm or irritant-induced asthma. The symptoms and findings are indicative of acute bronchoconstriction.

Given the acute nature and the presence of wheezing, the most appropriate initial step is to address the bronchoconstriction with a bronchodilator. Inhaled albuterol is a first-line treatment for acute bronchospasm as it provides rapid relief by relaxing the smooth muscles of the airways.

- Option A (Inhaled albuterol administration) is correct because it directly treats the bronchospasm.
- Option B (Bronchoscopic evaluation) is not indicated initially as there is no evidence of foreign body aspiration or structural abnormality requiring immediate visualization.
- Option C (Chest CT) is not urgent; the chest x-ray is sufficient and shows no acute pathology needing further imaging.
- Option D (Endotracheal intubation) is not necessary as the patient is not in respiratory failure (normal PCO2, no use of accessory muscles, stable vitals).
- Option E (Initiate methylprednisolone) may be considered for inflammation but is not the initial step; bronchodilators are used first for acute symptoms.

Thus, inhaled albuterol is the most appropriate initial management.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
An 87-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 4-day history of moderate abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting. 
He has not had a bowel movement or passed gas in the past 24 hours. He has colon cancer metastatic to the lungs, liver, and peritoneum. Eighteen months ago, he underwent a low anterior resection for T3N2 rectal cancer. Six months ago, exploratory 
laparotomy showed diffuse carcinomatosis and bowel obstruction, and an ileotransverse bypass was performed. The patient and 
his family were informed of the findings and advised that he would not be a candidate for a laparotomy in the future because 
there would be no surgical options to treat another obstruction. The patient has no other history of serious illness. Medications 
are 5-fluorouracil and bevacizumab. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood 
pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and mildly tender to palpation. There are no peritoneal signs. CT scan of 
the abdomen shows a mid-jejunal small-bowel obstruction, multiple intraperitoneal tumor deposits, and ascites. The physician recommends insertion of nasogastric and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes. The patient and his family request that the 
patient undergo a laparotomy and intestinal bypass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Proceed with the surgical intervention as requested by the patient and his family
options.C: Clarify to the patient and his family that the surgical procedure would not achieve its intended therapeutic goal.
options.D: Arrange for the patient's care to be continued at a different hospital
options.E: Recommend initiating palliative radiotherapy to the abdomen

results.correct_answer: Clarify to the patient and his family that the surgical procedure would not achieve its intended therapeutic goal.
results.predicted_answer: Clarify to the patient and his family that the surgical procedure would not achieve its intended therapeutic goal.
results.response:
The patient has a history of colon cancer with diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis and previously underwent an ileotransverse bypass for bowel obstruction. He now presents with another small-bowel obstruction. The physician recommends palliative measures (nasogastric and PEG tubes) to manage symptoms without surgery, given the advanced disease and prior indication that no further surgical options exist. The patient and family are requesting laparotomy and intestinal bypass, which was previously advised against due to the lack of surgical benefit in the setting of diffuse carcinomatosis.

The most appropriate step is to clarify that surgery would not achieve its intended therapeutic goal. This is because the obstruction is due to progressive metastatic disease, and previous surgery already found diffuse carcinomatosis, making another bypass unlikely to be successful or beneficial. Surgery in this context could cause significant morbidity without improving outcomes, and palliative conservative management is standard. It is important to address the family's concerns with clear communication about the prognosis and the goals of care, rather than proceeding with non-beneficial surgery, changing chemotherapy (which wouldn't resolve the acute obstruction), transferring care (which avoids the issue), or using palliative radiotherapy (which is not typically effective for bowel obstruction in this setting).

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 62-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of anxiety, insomnia, and frequent bowel movements. 
She has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss during this period. Pulse is 90/min and irregularly irregular, respirations are 22/min, and 
blood pressure is 150/65 mm Hg. She is restless. The thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged. A bruit is heard over the thyroid. 
Examination shows palmar erythema; some of her fingernails are separated from the nail beds. There is widening of the palpebral fissures and thickening of the skin over the dorsum of the feet with a peau d'orange appearance. There is a fine tremor. Which of 
the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla

results.response:
The patient's symptoms (anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, frequent bowel movements, irregularly irregular pulse) and signs (diffuse goiter with bruit, palmar erythema, onycholysis, lid retraction, pretibial myxedema, tremor) are classic for hyperthyroidism, specifically Graves disease.  

The irregularly irregular pulse suggests atrial fibrillation, which can be a complication of hyperthyroidism. Pretibial myxedema and thyroid acropachy (though not mentioned explicitly, the nail and skin changes fit) are also specific to Graves disease.  

Other options are less likely:  
- Adrenal insufficiency would cause weight loss but not this thyroid-related hyperactivity or eye/skin signs.  
- Carcinoid syndrome involves flushing, diarrhea, but not thyroid enlargement or hypermetabolic signs.  
- Hypercortisolism causes weight gain, not weight loss.  
- Pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension and symptoms, not sustained thyroid-related signs.  

<answer>E</answer>  
<belief>0.99</belief>

36.1°C

lower quadrant without rebound or guarding; no hepatosplenomegaly 
 
Diagnostic Studies 
• colonoscopy: areas of coalesced ulcers with normal appearing mucosa between, involving the ileum 
 
Question: Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient considering his condition?

options.A: Annual surveillance for the development of lymphoproliferative disease

results.response:
The colonoscopy finding of coalesced ulcers in the ileum with normal intervening mucosa is characteristic of Crohn disease.  
The patient has a significant smoking history (2 packs/day for 10 years), which is a strong risk factor for Crohn disease and is associated with worse disease severity and increased need for surgery. Smoking cessation is a key modifiable factor in management.  

The other options are not appropriate:  
- A: There is no indication for annual lymphoproliferative disease surveillance at this time.  
- B: Citalopram is not linked to Crohn disease and is for anxiety; stopping it without psychiatric input is not indicated.  
- C: Prophylactic colectomy is not indicated for ileal Crohn disease.  

Thus, the most appropriate recommendation is smoking cessation.

<answer>D</answer>

results.question:
A 19-year-old man is brought to the emergency department (ED) by ambulance 15 minutes after he was stabbed in the chest 
during a fight. The stab wound is located medially to the left nipple. At the scene, pulse was 105/min, respirations were 18/min, 
and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air showed an oxygen saturation of 96%. Two large-bore 
intravenous catheters were placed en route to the ED, and lactated Ringer solution was initiated. On arrival in the trauma bay, the 
patient develops a pulse of 130/min and blood pressure of 80/40 mm Hg. There is jugular venous distention. Results of focused 
assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST) are most likely to lead to which of the following next steps for this patient?

options.A: Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the chest and abdomen
options.C: Pericardiocentesis followed by insertion of a chest tube

results.predicted_answer: Pericardiocentesis followed by insertion of a chest tube
results.response:
The patient has a stab wound medial to the left nipple, which is concerning for cardiac injury.  
The rapid development of hypotension, tachycardia, jugular venous distention, and no mention of breath sounds being absent suggests cardiac tamponade rather than a simple tension pneumothorax (though both can cause JVD and hypotension, tamponade typically has muffled heart sounds and no improvement with chest tube alone).  

FAST exam in this scenario would likely show pericardial fluid.  
The next step for unstable traumatic cardiac tamponade is pericardiocentesis to temporize, followed by definitive surgical repair (e.g., thoracotomy or sternotomy).  
Option C says "Pericardiocentesis followed by insertion of a chest tube" — the chest tube might be placed for possible hemothorax, but the key is pericardiocentesis first for tamponade.  
Option D is only aspiration without mention of chest tube, but in trauma, chest tube is often placed for possible pleural blood.  
Given common USMLE teaching, for unstable traumatic tamponade, pericardiocentesis is an immediate life-saving procedure, and chest tube may be placed for associated hemothorax.  

I believe C is the intended answer.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 47-year-old woman is scheduled to undergo right knee arthroscopy and partial medial meniscectomy. Medical history is 
otherwise unremarkable. At her preoperative examination 2 weeks ago, her surgeon discussed the risks and benefits of the 
procedure, and both parties signed the operative consent form. On the patient's arrival at the hospital, the paperwork is reviewed. 
She is taken into the operating room and sedated, and the anesthesiology team starts a spinal anesthetic. After the patient's right 
lower extremity is prepped for the procedure, a surgical pause is taken to review the patient's name and consent form and to 
confirm the site of surgery. The operating room team is unable to locate the consent form for review. No markings are present on the patient's extremities to indicate the site of the procedure. The surgeon says that he remembers reviewing the consent form 
with the patient and seeing her sign it. The patient's husband is in the waiting room. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?

options.A: Confirm with the sedated patient if she recalls signing the consent form and proceed with the surgery if she affirms.
options.B: Have the patient sign a fresh consent form and then continue with the surgery.
options.C: Cancel the procedure and transfer the patient to the recovery area
options.D: Proceed with the surgery after securing verbal consent from the patient's husband.
options.E: Continue with the scheduled surgery without delay

results.correct_answer: Cancel the procedure and transfer the patient to the recovery area
results.predicted_answer: Cancel the procedure and transfer the patient to the recovery area
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 30-year-old man comes to the physician because he and his wife have been unable to conceive during the past 2 years. Previous 
evaluation of his wife showed no abnormalities. He has not been exposed to pesticides, heavy metals, radiation, or testicular 
overheating. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show a testosterone concentration within the lower reference range. A diagnosis of oligospermia is made. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial action by the physician?

options.A: Inquire about the patient's history of alcohol consumption, tobacco use, and psychological stressors
options.B: Counsel the patient on the inverse correlation between advancing age and sperm concentration
options.C: Initiate therapy with a medication that inhibits phosphodiesterase
options.E: Advise proceeding with in vitro fertilization.

results.correct_answer: Inquire about the patient's history of alcohol consumption, tobacco use, and psychological stressors
results.predicted_answer: Inquire about the patient's history of alcohol consumption, tobacco use, and psychological stressors
results.response:
The patient has oligospermia with low-normal testosterone and no obvious toxic exposures or physical exam abnormalities.  
The next step is to evaluate for reversible lifestyle factors (alcohol, smoking, stress) and medical conditions before considering assisted reproduction or empiric drug therapy.  
Testosterone replacement is contraindicated because it can suppress spermatogenesis.  

<answer>A</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 40-year-old man comes to the office during the summer because of a 3-day history of fever, diffuse muscle aches, fatigue, and 
sore throat. He has had no sick contacts. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. He received the influenza 
virus vaccine this year. Ten days ago, he returned from a trip to Malaysia, where he says he enjoyed the local food and nightlife. Temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 126/70 mm Hg. The remainder 
of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Blood    
   
   
   
  Hemoglobin  10 g/dL  
MCV  90 μm3 
WBC  2400/mm3 
   
   Neutrophils, segmented  60% 
Neutrophils, bands  5% 
Lymphocytes  5% 
Monocytes  20% 
Eosinophils  4% 
Basophils  6% 
Platelet count  350,000/mm3 
Red cell distribution width  14% 
 
Monospot test result is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Serologic evaluation for Epstein-Barr virus infection
options.C: Detection of antibodies against viral hemagglutinin antigens
options.D: HIV viral load quantification via nucleic acid amplification
options.E: Serologic testing for hepatitis B surface antigen

results.correct_answer: HIV viral load quantification via nucleic acid amplification
results.predicted_answer: HIV viral load quantification via nucleic acid amplification
results.response:
The patient has fever, sore throat, fatigue, and labs show pancytopenia (mild anemia, leukopenia with neutropenia and lymphopenia, though platelets are normal), plus monocytosis and atypical presentation post-travel.  
Monospot is negative, making typical infectious mononucleosis (EBV) less likely, though not impossible.  
Given the travel history (Malaysia), possible exposure risks, and pancytopenia with monocytosis, acute HIV infection (which can present with a mononucleosis-like illness and pancytopenia) should be considered.  
The most appropriate next step in diagnosis for acute HIV is HIV viral load via nucleic acid amplification, because serology may still be negative in the early weeks.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his girlfriend 30 minutes after she found him unconscious in his 
home. She last saw him yesterday and he appeared well. He has a history of cocaine and heroin use, and also use of prescription 
narcotic analgesics that he obtains from friends. On arrival, he is lethargic. Temperature is 35.0°C (95.0°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 8/min, and blood pressure is 96/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96%. The 
left lower extremity is cyanotic and cold to the touch. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of serum studies are shown: 
 
K+ 5.5 mEq/L  
HCO 3− 18 mEq/L  
Creatinine  1 mg/dL  
Creatine kinase  50,000 U/L  
 
This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following conditions?
results.response:
The patient has a history of heroin and cocaine use and presents with lethargy, hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, and respiratory depression — consistent with opioid overdose.  
The key finding is a cold, cyanotic left lower extremity, suggesting acute limb ischemia, likely from compartment syndrome or arterial occlusion (possibly due to prolonged immobilization during overdose).  
The labs show hyperkalemia (K+ 5.5 mEq/L), low bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis), and a very high creatine kinase (50,000 U/L) indicating severe rhabdomyolysis.  

Rhabdomyolysis puts him at high risk for **acute renal failure** due to myoglobin-induced tubular injury, especially in the setting of hypotension and acidosis.  

While hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis can cause ventricular dysrhythmias, the question asks for the condition he is at *greatest risk* for — acute renal failure is the most direct and common complication in this scenario.  

<answer>A</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 62-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department (ED) by her husband because of a 3-day history of increasing 
confusion. She has a 20-year history of bipolar disorder well controlled with medication for the past 5 years. Her husband forgot 
to bring her medication bottles and cannot remember which medication she takes. He reports that she has recently had mild 
headaches and has taken acetaminophen as needed. On questioning, he says that there has been no particular change in their 
routine, but during the past 2 days they have been outside playing tennis more than usual because of the onset of sunny, warm 
weather. While in the ED, the patient has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m
2. Pulse is 104/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. 
Examination shows dry mucous membranes. ECG shows a second-degree atrioventricular block. Which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's confusion?
results.response:
The patient has a history of bipolar disorder, recent increased outdoor activity in warm weather (risk for dehydration), dry mucous membranes, seizure, AV block on ECG, and confusion.  
Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index, and dehydration can precipitate toxicity. Toxicity symptoms include confusion, seizures, and cardiac conduction abnormalities like AV block.  
The question asks what is most likely responsible for her confusion — that points to lithium toxicity, not just lithium itself.  

<answer>C</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 32-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because she has been unable to conceive during the past 12 months. She 
and her husband have unprotected sexual intercourse three times weekly. Two years ago, she was involved in a motor vehicle collision; CT scan at that time showed absence of the left kidney. She has no other history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She has no known allergies. Vital signs are within normal limits. Pelvic examination shows no 
abnormalities. Hysterosalpingography is shown. When this patient conceives, she is at greatest risk for which of the following pregnancy complications?

options.A: Excessive fetal growth resulting in a large-for-gestational-age infant

results.response:
The patient has a history of unilateral renal agenesis (absence of the left kidney) discovered after a motor vehicle collision. Unilateral renal agenesis is often associated with congenital anomalies of the reproductive tract, particularly Müllerian duct anomalies. The hysterosalpingography (not shown but implied) likely reveals a uterine anomaly such as a unicornuate uterus, which is commonly linked with renal agenesis.

A unicornuate uterus is associated with several obstetric complications, including:
- Preterm delivery: Due to reduced uterine capacity and abnormal uterine shape, there is an increased risk of preterm labor and delivery.
- Other risks include miscarriage, fetal malpresentation, and intrauterine growth restriction, but preterm delivery is a classic and common complication.

Now evaluating the options:
A. Excessive fetal growth: Not typical; instead, intrauterine growth restriction may occur.
B. Multifetal pregnancy: Not directly associated with uterine anomalies; spontaneous multifetal pregnancy is not increased.
C. Decreased amniotic fluid volume (oligohydramnios): This is more related to fetal renal issues or placental insufficiency, not maternal renal agenesis or uterine anomalies.
D. Gestational hypertension with proteinuria (preeclampsia): There is no direct link between uterine anomalies or renal agenesis and preeclampsia.
E. Preterm delivery: This is the most common and significant risk associated with a unicornuate uterus.

Thus, the greatest risk for this patient is preterm delivery.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

• Appearance: sitting in a chair; comfortable, not in acute distress

Question: An adverse effect of which of the following medications is the most likely cause of this patient's current condition?
results.response:
The patient has orthostatic hypotension (BP drops from 115/70 lying to 95/55 standing), with symptoms of light-headedness, tunnel vision, and near-syncope.  
Clonidine is an alpha-2 agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow, which can cause hypotension, especially orthostatic hypotension, and is a known side effect.  
The recent initiation of clonidine (10 days ago) aligns with the timing of symptoms.  
Other medications like rifampin (enzyme inducer) or albuterol (beta-agonist) are less likely to cause this pattern of orthostasis as a new issue compared to clonidine.

results.question:
A 47-year-old woman comes to the office for a follow-up examination. Three days ago, she was brought to the emergency 
department because of cellulitis of her right lower extremity, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy was begun. She has had no adverse reaction to the medication. She has kept her leg elevated, and her cellulitis has improved moderately. She also has hypertension, gout, and congenital heart disease. Her other medications are lisinopril, allopurinol, and warfarin. Five years 
ago, she underwent mechanical mitral valve replacement. Temperature today is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 128/83 mm Hg. Examination shows a 4 × 5-cm, erythematous, warm rash over the right lower extremity. There is no 
red streaking, vesicles, or purulence. There is no rash elsewhere. Pulses in the lower extremities are intact. Lungs are clear to 
auscultation. On cardiac examination, a mechanical click is heard during S
1. A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the 
apex. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Blood    
  
   Hematocrit  35% 
WBC  13,000/mm3 
Platelet count  357,000/mm3 
PTT 38 sec  
PT 57 sec (INR=5.2)  
 
Which of the following actions is most likely to have prevented the near miss in this patient?

options.A: Check for possible interactions between medications
options.D: Doppler ultrasound evaluation of the deep veins in the right leg

results.correct_answer: Check for possible interactions between medications
results.predicted_answer: Check for possible interactions between medications
results.response:
The patient is on warfarin and has an INR of 5.2, which is supratherapeutic and increases bleeding risk.  
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole inhibits warfarin metabolism and can markedly increase INR.  
Checking for drug interactions before starting a new medication could have identified this risk and allowed for warfarin dose adjustment or antibiotic choice change.  

<answer>A</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 58-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine examination. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. During the past 3 months, 
her fingerstick blood glucose concentrations have ranged from 80 mg/dL to 230 mg/dL; previously, they ranged from 80 mg/dL to 140 mg/dL. Medications are metformin and aspirin. During the past 3 months, she has exercised three times weekly; previously, 
she exercised once weekly. She follows a balanced diet. Despite exercise and diet, she has not had weight loss. She is 157 cm (5 
ft 2 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m
2. Examination shows a 1 × 2-cm, erythematous, soft lesion over the 
posterior plantar aspect of the left foot. Sensation to pinprick and touch is decreased over the ankles and feet. Hemoglobin A 1c is 
7%. Which of the following findings are most likely to be present in this patient? 
 
     
 Glucose  Insulin  Insulin Receptor  
Responsiveness

options.A: Elevated glucose levels, elevated insulin levels, and reduced insulin receptor responsiveness
options.B: Elevated glucose levels, elevated insulin levels, and normal insulin receptor responsiveness
options.C: Elevated glucose levels, normal insulin levels, reduced insulin receptor sensitivity
options.D: Elevated glucose levels, insulin secretion within normal limits, and insulin receptor function unimpaired
options.E: Glucose levels are within normal limits, insulin secretion is unimpaired, and insulin receptor sensitivity is preserved.

results.correct_answer: Elevated glucose levels, elevated insulin levels, and reduced insulin receptor responsiveness
results.predicted_answer: Elevated glucose levels, elevated insulin levels, and reduced insulin receptor responsiveness
results.response:
The patient has type 2 diabetes with worsening glycemic control (HbA1c 7%, but glucose readings up to 230 mg/dL) despite lifestyle efforts.  
In type 2 diabetes, insulin resistance leads to elevated glucose → increased insulin secretion initially, but over time beta-cell function declines.  
Here, she likely still has significant insulin resistance (obesity, clinical findings of diabetes complications) but may have relative insulin deficiency for the level of glycemia.  
Given the hyperglycemia and pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes, the most consistent picture is: elevated glucose, elevated or normal insulin (but insufficient to overcome resistance), and reduced insulin receptor responsiveness (insulin resistance).  
Option A fits best: elevated glucose, elevated insulin (compensatory hyperinsulinemia early/mid disease), reduced insulin receptor responsiveness.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 39-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of moderate pain and swelling of his right 
knee and ankle. His symptoms began after he scaled a wall during an obstacle course. He landed awkwardly on his right leg and 
rolled his right ankle; his knee buckled. He limped to the finish line. He underwent reconstruction of a torn right anterior cruciate ligament 15 years ago. He and his boyfriend are engaged to be married. He feels safe at home. He does not appear to be in distress. 
Examination of the right knee shows mild tenderness to palpation of the midsagittal joint line. Adduction and abduction stress testing is stable at 0 and 30 degrees of flexion. Range of motion is full. Lachman test is negative. There is moderate tenderness 
to palpation of the distal aspect of the fibula. There are ecchymoses over the anterolateral aspect of the right ankle. The ankle is 
swollen. There is no tenderness to palpation of the medial aspect of the ankle. Range of motion of the ankle is limited to 5 degrees 
of dorsiflexion and 30 degrees of plantar flexion because of pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in 
diagnosis? 
 
 Knee  Ankle

options.A: Obtain radiographs of both the knee and ankle.
options.C: No imaging studies are warranted for the knee; however, radiography should be obtained for the ankle.
options.D: No imaging or laboratory studies are required for either joint
