results.question:
A 13-year-old girl is brought to the office for evaluation of short stature. Since birth, she has been below the 3rd percentile for 
height and at the 3rd percentile for weight. She has never had a menstrual period. There is no family history of short stature, but her maternal uncle did not begin puberty until the age of 16 years. Her current height is consistent with that of a 9½-year-old girl. Pulse is 82/min and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Sexual maturity rating is stage 1 for breast development and stage 2 for 
pubic hair development. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Obtain a complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel
options.B: Obtain a urinary free cortisol level
options.C: Obtain a radiograph of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment
options.D: Obtain radiographs of the appendicular skeleton and vertebral column

results.correct_answer: Obtain a radiograph of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment
results.predicted_answer: Obtain a radiograph of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 20-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-year history of a lump on her left ear that appeared 2 months after she 
had the ear pierced. Physical examination shows the findings in the photograph. Which of the following is the most appropriate 
initial treatment for this patient?
results.response:
The photograph (not provided, but based on the clinical scenario) likely shows a keloid on the ear, which is a common complication after ear piercing. Keloids are benign fibrous growths that result from an overgrowth of scar tissue. For initial treatment of a keloid, intralesional corticosteroid administration (such as triamcinolone) is the first-line therapy. It helps to reduce the size and symptoms of the keloid by inhibiting collagen synthesis and inflammation.

Other options are not typically first-line:
- B. Application of liquid nitrogen (cryotherapy) is used but often in combination with intralesional steroids, not alone initially.
- C. Laser therapy is considered for refractory cases.
- D. Aspiration with a needle is not effective for keloids as they are solid masses.
- E. Surgical excision with adequate margins has a high recurrence rate if not combined with other therapies (e.g., steroids or radiation) and is not initial treatment.

Thus, the most appropriate initial treatment is intralesional corticosteroid administration.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 42-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 20 minutes after a head-on motor vehicle collision 
in which she was the restrained driver. On arrival, she is alert and reports chest and abdominal pain. Medical history is 
unremarkable and she takes no medications. Temperature is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood 
pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 4 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 90%. Breath 
sounds are decreased on the right. Trachea is in the midline position. Cardiac examination discloses no abnormalities. An abrasion consistent in shape with a seat belt is present over the right upper abdominal quadrant; the area is tender to palpation. Radial and 
dorsalis pedis pulses are normal bilaterally. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Arterial blood gas analysis on oxygen at 4 L/min  
  
 PO2 65 mm Hg  
PCO2 32 mm Hg  
pH 7.38 
Blood    
 Hemoglobin  10.0 g/dL  
 
Chest x-ray is shown. In addition to intravenous analgesic therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Endotracheal intubation with initiation of positive pressure ventilation
options.C: Insertion of an epidural catheter into the thoracic spine
options.D: Needle decompression of the pleural space
options.G: Close monitoring with no further intervention

results.response:
The patient has decreased breath sounds on the right, hypoxia, and a chest x-ray consistent with a large right hemothorax (likely from the description).  
In trauma with hemothorax, the next step after diagnosis is chest tube insertion (tube thoracostomy) to drain blood, re-expand the lung, and monitor bleeding.  
Needle decompression is for tension pneumothorax, which she doesn’t have (midline trachea, stable BP). Intubation may be needed later but not yet.  

<answer>F</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 31-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of intermittent nonbloody diarrhea. The first episode 
began 5 days after a camping trip in rural Virginia with her family. During the trip, she drank from a spring-fed pond that she 
says was “clean.” During the past 3 weeks, she also has had a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. She has not 
had fever or other gastrointestinal symptoms. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is sexually active 
with one female partner. The patient is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (119 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.9°C 
(100.2°F), pulse is 85/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination 
shows mild, diffuse tenderness to deep palpation; bowel sounds are increased. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. 
Results of a stool smear are shown. Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?
results.response:
<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A randomized placebo-controlled clinical trial is conducted to assess the effectiveness of a selective estrogen receptor modulator 
(SERM) for chemoprophylaxis of breast cancer. A subset of the results shows: 
 
  SERM  Placebo  
Number of participants  2400  1200  
Woman -years of follow -up 7200  3600  
Invasive breast cancer  7 13 
Breast cancer rate (per 1000 woman -years)  1 3.6 
Estrogen receptor status (invasive cancers)      
 Positive  2 10 
 Negative  4 2 
 Unknown  1 1 
 
Which of the following represents the absolute risk reduction for invasive breast cancer per 1000 woman-years in those treated 
with a SERM?

options.D: The absolute risk reduction is 2.6 per 1000 woman-years.

results.response:
The absolute risk reduction (ARR) is calculated as the difference in event rates between the control group and the treatment group.  

Control group (placebo) rate = 3.6 per 1000 woman-years  
Treatment group (SERM) rate = 1.0 per 1000 woman-years  

ARR = 3.6 − 1.0 = 2.6 per 1000 woman-years.  

The question asks for the ARR **per 1000 woman-years**, so the correct choice is the one that states exactly that without adding extra incorrect wording like "fewer cases" (which is implied but not part of the standard ARR unit expression in this exam context).  

Looking at the options:  
- A says "2.6 per 1000 woman-years" → correct and precise.  
- B says "2.6 fewer cases per 1000 woman-years" → redundant wording ("fewer cases" is not standard for ARR units in epidemiology reporting).  
- C says "2.6" → incomplete, missing units.  
- D is a full sentence, but the question likely wants the concise exam answer like A.  
- E is incorrect value.  

In USMLE style, the best match is **A** because it gives the number and unit exactly as used in the table.  

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 1 hour after vomiting bright red blood. She thinks it was approximately 1 
cup of blood. She reports that during the past month, she has felt full sooner than usual while eating. She has not had other episodes of 
vomiting and has not had weight loss. She has no history of operative procedures. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. 
Medications are hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She has not taken aspirin or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the past 
month. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use other substances. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 116/min, 
respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Examination shows mild tenderness to palpation in the midepigastric 
area; there is no rebound tenderness. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Hematocrit is 31%, platelet count is 240,000/mm3, 
and prothrombin time is 13 seconds (INR=1.2).

In addition to intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, which of the following is the most appropriate 
next step in management?

options.D: Evaluation of the colon with endoscopic visualization

results.response:
The patient presents with an acute upper GI bleed (hematemesis, positive occult blood, epigastric symptoms) and is hemodynamically stable after initial resuscitation.  
The most appropriate next diagnostic and therapeutic step is **upper gastrointestinal endoscopy** because it can identify the source (likely a peptic ulcer, gastritis, or Mallory-Weiss tear) and allow for intervention (e.g., coagulation, clipping) if active bleeding is found.  

Other options:  
- CT abdomen (A) is less sensitive for mucosal bleeding sources.  
- Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (B) is for neuroendocrine tumors, not acute bleed.  
- Radionuclide imaging (C) is for slow, obscure bleeding, not acute hematemesis.  
- Colonoscopy (D) is for suspected lower GI bleeding.  
- Mesenteric arteriography (F) is for massive bleeding when endoscopy fails.  

<answer>E</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 1 hour after vomiting bright red blood. She thinks it was approximately 1 
cup of blood. She reports that during the past month, she has felt full sooner than usual while eating. She has not had other episodes of 
vomiting and has not had weight loss. She has no history of operative procedures. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. 
Medications are hydrochlorothiazide and metformin. She has not taken aspirin or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the past 
month. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use other substances. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 116/min, 
respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Examination shows mild tenderness to palpation in the midepigastric 
area; there is no rebound tenderness. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Hematocrit is 31%, platelet count is 240,000/mm3, 
and prothrombin time is 13 seconds (INR=1.2).

After intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, the patient undergoes esophagogastroduodenoscopy. A 
photograph of the endoscopic findings is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Initiation of a proton pump inhibitor regimen
options.C: Initiating treatment with a tyrosine kinase inhibitor

results.response:
The patient presents with acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding (hematemesis), epigastric fullness, and tenderness, along with signs of hypovolemia (tachycardia, relatively low BP) and anemia. The positive stool occult blood confirms GI bleeding. Endoscopy is performed, and the description implies a finding that requires management. Given the history of early satiety and no NSAID use, along with hypertension and diabetes (which are risk factors for vascular diseases), the most likely endoscopic finding is a bleeding ulcer or vascular lesion. However, the options suggest specific interventions.

Reviewing the options:
- A. Octreotide: Used for variceal bleeding, but there is no history of liver disease or alcohol use, making varices unlikely.
- B. Proton pump inhibitor (PPI): Useful for ulcer bleeding but typically after endoscopic therapy if high-risk stigmata are present.
- C. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor: Used for gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs), which can present with bleeding and early satiety. The endoscopic photograph might show a submucosal mass suggestive of GIST.
- D. Endoscopic intervention (e.g., clipping, coagulation): Standard for active bleeding from ulcers or vascular lesions.
- E. Endoscopic tissue sampling: Biopsy would be appropriate if a mass or suspicious lesion is seen.
- F. Endoscopic removal: Would be for polyps or small lesions.

The key clue is "early satiety" and no NSAID use, which raises suspicion for a gastric tumor like GIST. GISTs are common in the stomach and can present with bleeding and early satiety. Endoscopic photograph likely shows a submucosal mass with ulceration and bleeding. For a bleeding GIST, endoscopic intervention to achieve hemostasis (D) is critical acutely, but tissue sampling (E) is needed for diagnosis. However, the question asks for the "most appropriate next step in management" after endoscopy, which for a bleeding mass would first require hemostasis to stabilize the patient, followed by biopsy.

But note: tyrosine kinase inhibitors (e.g., imatinib) are used for GISTs, but only after histological confirmation. Without tissue diagnosis, initiating such treatment is premature.

Given the acute bleeding, endoscopic hemostasis (D) is the immediate next step to control bleeding, regardless of the cause. This is standard for active bleeding lesions seen on endoscopy.

However, if the lesion is a GIST, endoscopic removal (F) is not typically done as GISTs are submucosal and require surgical resection; endoscopic resection is only for small lesions (<2 cm) and with specific techniques, but it's not first-line for bleeding.

The most appropriate is endoscopic intervention to achieve hemostasis (D) for acute bleeding, which can be done with clips or coagulation. Then, tissue sampling can be performed during the same procedure.

But option E (endoscopic tissue sampling) might be done simultaneously, but hemostasis takes priority in active bleeding.

Given the options, D is correct for acute management.

But let's see the belief: The history strongly suggests a gastric tumor (early satiety, no NSAIDs), so biopsy is key. However, with active bleeding, hemostasis is urgent.

In standard guidelines, for non-variceal UGIB, endoscopic therapy is indicated for high-risk stigmata (active bleeding, visible vessel). So D is appropriate.

For GISTs, if bleeding, endoscopic therapy can be used temporarily, but surgery is definitive.

But the question says "next step", so hemostasis.

Option C (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) is wrong without biopsy.

So, D is correct.

However, if the endoscopic photo shows a typical ulcer with bleeding, D is correct. If it shows a mass, E might be needed, but hemostasis first.

Given the acuity, D is most appropriate.

Belief: 0.95 (high, as endoscopic hemostasis is standard for active bleeding lesions).
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 77-year-old woman who resides in a skilled nursing care facility is examined by the physician 1 day after staff noticed some 
blood on her bedsheets. She sustained a cerebral infarction 8 years ago and has been confined to bed since then because of residual 
left hemiparesis. She is currently taking warfarin. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. 
Temperature is 36.0°C (96.8°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 180/90 mm Hg. There is no 
lymphadenopathy. Examination shows a small, clean, decubitus ulcer on the sacrum. Pelvic examination shows a 3-cm, raised, 
fleshy lesion on the right labium majus. Vaginal examination cannot be performed because of the patient's discomfort. Which of 
the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Administration of a topical antifungal medication
options.D: Administration of a topical corticosteroid preparation
options.F: Excisional biopsy of the vulvar lesion

results.correct_answer: Excisional biopsy of the vulvar lesion
results.response:
The patient is a 77-year-old woman with multiple risk factors for vulvar cancer, including advanced age, chronic immobility, and a history of long-term warfarin use (which may be for atrial fibrillation related to her past cerebral infarction, but this is not specified). She presents with a 3-cm raised, fleshy lesion on the right labium majus, which is suspicious for malignancy. Vulvar cancer is often diagnosed late in elderly patients due to delayed presentation. The most common type is squamous cell carcinoma, and biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis. Given the lesion's characteristics and the patient's risk factors, excisional biopsy is the most appropriate next step to obtain a definitive histopathological diagnosis. Other options are less appropriate: vulvar cytology (A) is not reliable for diagnosis; imaging (B) might be used for staging but not before biopsy; antifungal (C) or corticosteroid (D) would be for benign conditions like infection or inflammation, which are less likely here; colonoscopy (E) is unrelated to vulvar lesions unless there is suspicion of metastatic disease, but primary assessment is needed first.

Belief in this answer is high due to the clear clinical suspicion of vulvar cancer and the standard approach to biopsy such lesions.
<answer>F</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 68-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of moderate low back pain that extends into both legs. He 
first noticed the pain while standing in the ticket line at a theater. At that time, the pain was accompanied by tingling in both legs. 
The symptoms recurred a few days later as he was standing in the grocery checkout line. The pain has not responded to ibuprofen 
or naproxen but improves with sitting or lying down. Prior to onset of the pain, the patient typically walked for 30 minutes four 
times weekly. He discontinued his walking regimen 2 months ago because it seemed to trigger his back pain. He has hypertension 
and hyperlipidemia. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene, simvastatin, and aspirin. The patient has smoked one pack 
of cigarettes daily for 40 years. Pulse is 74/min and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation; expiration 
phase is delayed. On cardiac examination, a grade 2/6 systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur is heard at the upper right sternal 
border. There is trace ankle edema bilaterally; ankle brachial index is 1.0. Musculoskeletal and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Abdominal aortic dilatation with risk of rupture
options.E: Intermittent claudication due to peripheral arterial disease

results.response:
The patient’s symptoms of low back and bilateral leg pain triggered by standing or walking (neurogenic claudication), relieved by sitting or lying down (flexion relieves symptoms), in an older adult with a history of smoking, are classic for lumbar spinal stenosis.  

The normal ankle-brachial index and lack of true peripheral arterial disease symptoms rule out intermittent claudication from PAD. No trauma or acute collapse makes osteoporotic fracture less likely. Neurologic exam is normal, making bilateral lower extremity neuropathy unlikely. Abdominal aortic dilatation isn’t suggested by the presentation.  

Thus, the most likely diagnosis is lumbar spinal stenosis.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 50-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-day history of mild headache and intermittent confusion. He has a 30-year 
history of schizophrenia and a 5-year history of emphysema. Medications are clozapine, sertraline, and inhaled beclomethasone 
and salmeterol. He also receives home oxygen therapy. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 33 years but quit 5 years ago. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), respirations are 20/min, pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Examination 
shows moist mucous membranes. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic examination shows 
no focal findings. Results of a complete blood count are within the reference ranges. Results of other laboratory studies are shown: 
 
Serum    
 
  
  
 Na+ 116 mEq/L  
K+ 4 mEq/L  
Cl− 90 mEq/L  
Urea nitrogen  10 mg/dL  
Creatinine  1 mg/dL  
Glucose  100 mg/dL  
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Assessment of cortisol and thyroid-stimulating hormone levels in the blood
options.C: Determination of serum uric acid levels
options.D: Determination of urine and plasma osmolality

results.correct_answer: Determination of urine and plasma osmolality
results.predicted_answer: Determination of urine and plasma osmolality
results.response:
The patient has severe hyponatremia (Na+ 116 mEq/L) with symptoms (headache, confusion). The first step is to determine the cause by assessing volume status and urine/plasma osmolality to differentiate between SIADH, hypovolemia, or other causes.  
He is on clozapine, which can cause SIADH, and has no signs of volume depletion (normal BP, moist mucous membranes, no edema, normal BUN/creatinine), so SIADH is likely. Urine and plasma osmolality will confirm this.  

Other tests (cortisol/TSH, uric acid, imaging) may be considered later but are not the most appropriate *next* step.  

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 67-year-old man is evaluated in the intensive care unit. He has end-stage pancreatic cancer and was hospitalized 3 days ago for 
treatment of pneumonia. Respirations are 6/min. Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen by face mask shows an oxygen saturation of 78%. Examination shows feeble respiratory efforts; he is using accessory muscles of respiration. On mental status examination, 
the patient is oriented to person but not to place or time. If the patient is not endotracheally intubated and mechanically ventilated, he will die within hours. His wife says the patient recently told her that he would never want mechanical ventilation, but they never completed paperwork regarding his wishes. His daughter insists that he be mechanically ventilated. Which of the following 
is the most appropriate action for the physician to take?

options.A: Initiate mechanical ventilation via endotracheal intubation
options.B: Proceed with endotracheal intubation and subsequently seek guidance from the hospital ethics committee concerning the continuation of mechanical ventilation.
options.D: Offer only supportive care focused on comfort and symptom management
options.E: Request judicial appointment of a surrogate decision-maker

results.correct_answer: Offer only supportive care focused on comfort and symptom management
results.predicted_answer: Offer only supportive care focused on comfort and symptom management
results.response:
The patient is in a critical condition with respiratory failure and altered mental status, and without mechanical ventilation, he will die within hours. The wife reports that the patient previously expressed he would never want mechanical ventilation, but there is no formal advance directive. The daughter insists on mechanical ventilation, creating a conflict among potential surrogates.

In such situations, the physician should prioritize the patient's known wishes. Since the wife is the next of kin and reports the patient's explicit refusal of mechanical ventilation, her account should be given significant weight, even without formal paperwork. Initiating mechanical ventilation against the patient's expressed wishes would be inappropriate and could constitute battery. The most appropriate action is to provide supportive care focused on comfort, as this aligns with the patient's stated preferences.

Option D is correct because it respects the patient's autonomy and previously expressed wishes, avoiding unwanted interventions. Options A, B, and C involve initiating mechanical ventilation, which contradicts the patient's stated desire. Option E (requesting a judicial appointment) is not immediately necessary as the wife is the likely surrogate, and given the urgency (death within hours), there is no time for legal proceedings.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 5-day-old boy is brought to the office for an initial well-child examination. He was born at 40 weeks' gestation and discharged 
at 60 hours of life. On newborn screening, hemoglobin electrophoresis showed an FS pattern. He is at the 50th percentile for 
length and weight. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 136/min, and respirations are 34/min. He appears well. Examination 
shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Initiate transfusion therapy on a monthly basis
options.E: Initiate prophylactic antibiotic therapy with penicillin

results.correct_answer: Initiate prophylactic antibiotic therapy with penicillin
results.predicted_answer: Initiate prophylactic antibiotic therapy with penicillin
results.response:
The FS pattern on hemoglobin electrophoresis indicates sickle cell disease (likely homozygous HbSS).  
For newborns diagnosed via screening, the standard next step is to start prophylactic penicillin by 2 months of age to prevent pneumococcal sepsis, but the question asks for the most appropriate next step now at 5 days old.  
Current guidelines recommend initiating prophylactic penicillin as soon as the diagnosis is confirmed, typically starting at diagnosis (by 2 months at latest), but often started at the first visit after confirmatory testing. Since he is well and just presenting for the first well visit after screening, starting penicillin prophylaxis is the correct step before complications arise.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 1-week-old female newborn is brought to the office for a follow-up examination after newborn screening showed a serum TSH 
concentration of 40 μU/mL (N=1–20). She has been breast-feeding well. She passes five to six stools and has multiple wet diapers 
daily. She has returned to her birth weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Serum studies today show a TSH concentration of 14 μU/mL (N=0.5–6.5) and free thyroxine (FT
4) concentration of 0.4 ng/dL (N=0.9–
2.2). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Regular monitoring of serum thyroglobulin levels on a monthly basis
options.D: Periodic monitoring of serum TSH and free T4 levels at monthly intervals

results.response:
Based on the clinical scenario, the newborn has abnormal thyroid function tests consistent with congenital hypothyroidism. The initial newborn screening showed an elevated TSH (40 μU/mL), and follow-up tests confirm a persistently elevated TSH (14 μU/mL, normal 0.5–6.5) and low free T4 (0.4 ng/dL, normal 0.9–2.2). Despite being asymptomatic (breastfeeding well, normal stool and urine output, normal vitals, and no abnormalities on exam), the biochemical evidence indicates primary hypothyroidism.

Congenital hypothyroidism requires prompt treatment to prevent neurodevelopmental deficits. The American Academy of Pediatrics guidelines recommend immediate initiation of levothyroxine therapy when confirmatory tests show low T4 and elevated TSH, even if the infant appears clinically normal. Delaying treatment can lead to irreversible intellectual disability.

Evaluating the options:
- A. Hydrocortisone is not indicated unless there is evidence of adrenal insufficiency (e.g., in cases of central hypothyroidism or combined pituitary deficiency), which is not suggested here.
- B. Initiation of levothyroxine is the standard of care for confirmed congenital hypothyroidism.
- C. Monitoring thyroglobulin is not routine; it is sometimes used to assess thyroid tissue in cases of dyshormonogenesis but is not a primary management step.
- D. Periodic monitoring without treatment is inappropriate due to the risk of neurodevelopmental damage.
- E. Thyroid scintigraphy can help determine the etiology (e.g., agenesis vs. dyshormonogenesis) but should not delay treatment. It can be considered after starting therapy.
- F. Thyroid ultrasonography is similarly useful for identifying structural abnormalities but is not urgent and should not postpone treatment.

The most appropriate next step is to start levothyroxine immediately to ensure optimal outcomes.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
An 18-year-old patient comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of severe abdominal pain. The pain began 
in the middle of the abdomen after a large meal but now is localized to the right lower quadrant. The patient also has a 2-month 
history of intermittent diarrhea. Three weeks ago, the patient had an upper respiratory tract infection that resolved spontaneously. 
The patient currently takes no medications. The patient identifies as nonbinary and uses they/they pronouns; their assigned sex 
at birth was male. They do not smoke cigarettes or use other substances. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows fullness and tenderness to palpation 
of the right lower quadrant. Leukocyte count is 11,000/mm^3. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in 
diagnosis?

options.E: Small bowel follow-through with upper gastrointestinal series

results.response:
The patient’s presentation (migration of pain from periumbilical to RLQ, fever, leukocytosis) suggests acute appendicitis.  
However, the 2-month history of intermittent diarrhea and recent URI raise the possibility of Crohn’s disease or infectious/inflammatory conditions.  
In a young adult with RLQ pain and possible Crohn’s, CT abdomen is more sensitive and specific than ultrasound for evaluating both appendicitis and inflammatory bowel disease, especially if there is abdominal fullness suggesting a possible mass or phlegmon.  
Ultrasound is often first in children or pregnant patients, but in an 18-year-old non-pregnant patient with possible complications, CT is preferred for definitive diagnosis.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 25-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, at 36 weeks’ gestation comes to the emergency department because of heavy vaginal 
bleeding following sexual intercourse 3 hours ago. She has received no prenatal care. She has no history of serious illness or 
operative procedures. Vital signs are within normal limits. Continuous external fetal heart monitoring shows a baseline of 155/min with no accelerations or decelerations. The uterus is consistent in size with a 36-week gestation. Tocodynamometer monitoring 
shows contractions every 8 minutes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
The patient presents at 36 weeks' gestation with heavy vaginal bleeding after sexual intercourse, which raises concern for placenta previa. The history of no prenatal care means this condition may not have been previously diagnosed. Vital signs are normal, and fetal heart monitoring is reassuring with a baseline of 155/min and no decelerations. Contractions are present every 8 minutes, but the bleeding is the primary issue. In cases of suspected placenta previa, any manipulation of the cervix (including a manual cervical assessment) is contraindicated because it can provoke severe hemorrhage. The appropriate next step is to confirm the placental location with abdominal ultrasonography, which is non-invasive and safe.

- Option A (immediate cesarean section) is not indicated without confirmation of placenta previa or fetal distress; the fetus is currently stable.
- Option B (manual cervical assessment) is absolutely contraindicated due to the risk of exacerbating bleeding.
- Option C (magnesium sulfate) is used for neuroprotection in preterm labor or eclampsia management, not for bleeding.
- Option D (oxytocin infusion) is used to augment labor, but here the bleeding may be due to previa, and inducing labor could be harmful.
- Option E (abdominal ultrasonography) is the correct step to evaluate placental location without risking further bleeding.

Thus, the most appropriate next step is to perform abdominal ultrasonography.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
A 75-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-day history of fever. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, 
and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. One year ago, he had a stroke with residual right lower extremity hemiparesis. 
Medications are amlodipine, tiotropium, metformin, glimepiride, lisinopril, and aspirin. He does not drink alcoholic beverages 
and has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years. The patient is widowed, lives alone, and has three adult children. He is 
not in acute distress. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), 
pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen 
saturation of 96%. Scattered end-expiratory wheezes are heard. There is dense hemiparesis of the right lower extremity. 
Appropriate therapy is initiated, and 3 days later, the patient's condition has improved. He is awake, alert, and conversing with one of his sons about current events. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Discharge plans are discussed with the patient, his son, the physician, and a physical therapist. The physical therapist says the 
patient would be best cared for in a nursing care facility. The patient acknowledges that a nursing facility would provide better care for him but says he would prefer to remain at home with a visiting nurse. Once outside the patient's room, his son says, "I 
have his power of attorney. Don't listen to him. He needs to be in a nursing home." Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Advise the son that the patient retains the authority to make this choice, even if it differs from the clinical recommendation.
options.B: Provide reassurance to the son that the patient will be placed in a nursing facility as advised by the physical therapist.
options.C: Arrange a meeting with all family members involved, including the son and the patient's other children, to discuss the plan of care before proceeding.
options.D: Assess the patient's decision-making capacity through a psychiatric consultation
options.E: Verify the documentation that grants the son power of attorney.

results.correct_answer: Advise the son that the patient retains the authority to make this choice, even if it differs from the clinical recommendation.
results.predicted_answer: Verify the documentation that grants the son power of attorney.
results.response:
<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 59-year -old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of weakness of his left arm and leg and 
mild headache. He says that, when he was gardening with his wife, he suddenly became unable to hold his gardening tool, lift his left arm, or walk. Two weeks ago, he had a nonproductive cough and nasal congestion that resolved spontaneously after 1 week. 
He has hypertension treated with lisinopril. On arrival, he is lethargic. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 97 kg (215 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m
2. Temperature is 36.6°C (97.9°F), pulse is 94/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 174/109 mm Hg. The 
pupils measure 3 mm and are reactive to light. Muscle movement is decreased over the left lower aspect of the face. Muscle 
strength is 0/5 in the left extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in the left extremities and 2+ in the right extremities. CT 
scan of the head shows a large area of increased attenuation in the right putamen region. Which of the following is the most likely 
cause of this patient’s condition?
results.response:

results.question:
A 24-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks’ gestation comes to the office for a scheduled dilatation and suction curettage. 
Ultrasonography 2 days ago showed a fetus consistent in size with a 9-week gestation with absent cardiac activity. Two weeks 
ago, the patient had an upper respiratory tract infection that resolved spontaneously. One year ago, she was diagnosed with herpes 
simplex virus (HSV) 2; her last outbreak was 6 months ago. She has no other history of serious illness. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) 
tall. She weighed 82 kg (180 lb) prior to her pregnancy; BMI was 31 kg/m2. She has had a 3.6-kg (8-lb) weight gain during her 
pregnancy. Vital signs are within normal limits. The vulvar area is shaven; no lesions are noted. Examination shows a normal-appearing cervix. The uterus is nontender and consistent in size with an 8-week gestation. There is homogenous white-gray 
vaginal discharge; the pH of the discharge is 5.5. She undergoes dilatation and suction curettage with sterile instruments and 
povidone-iodine vaginal cleansing. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing risk factor for postoperative infection in 
this patient?

options.D: Presence of homogenous white-gray vaginal discharge with a pH of 5.5

results.correct_answer: Presence of homogenous white-gray vaginal discharge with a pH of 5.5
results.predicted_answer: Presence of homogenous white-gray vaginal discharge with a pH of 5.5
results.response:
The white-gray vaginal discharge with pH 5.5 suggests bacterial vaginosis, which is a known risk factor for post-procedure endometritis after uterine instrumentation.  

Recent URI is unrelated. HSV-2 is not active and not a surgical site infection risk here. Elevated BMI increases infection risk in some surgeries but is less directly relevant than an active vaginal infection. Preoperative hair removal is not a strong risk if done by shaving without breaks in skin.  

The strongest predisposing factor is bacterial vaginosis.  

<answer>D</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 74-year-old woman comes to the clinic 2 days after the sudden onset of severe left foot pain. She has had difficulty walking 
during this time and has been using a cane. She has not sustained trauma to the foot. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus and 
hypertension. Medications are metformin, insulin glargine, hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. She typically wears 
support stockings because of lower leg swelling, but the foot pain has made it difficult for her to wear them recently. She is a traveling pastor and spends more than 4 hours daily in her car. She has been monogamous with her wife for the past 30 years. 
Blood pressure is 146/88 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the left great toe is shown. No other abnormalities are noted. Hemoglobin A
1c is 7.4%, serum creatinine concentration is 1.4 mg/dL, and serum uric acid concentration 
is 7.8 mg/dL. Results of serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most 
likely diagnosis?

options.A: Infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue
options.C: Septic bursitis of the interphalangeal joint

results.response:
The patient presents with sudden onset of severe left foot pain, specifically in the great toe, without trauma. Key points from the history include type 2 diabetes, hypertension, use of diuretic (hydrochlorothiazide, which can promote hyperuricemia), elevated serum uric acid (7.8 mg/dL, above normal range), and difficulty wearing support stockings due to pain. She spends long hours in the car, which may contribute to immobility and risk for gout. The photograph (not shown but implied) likely shows signs consistent with acute gouty arthritis, such as erythema, swelling, and tenderness of the great toe (classic podagra). 

Other options are less likely:
- A (Infection of dermis/subcutaneous tissue): No fever or signs of infection; pain is acute and severe but not described as spreading or with purulence.
- C (Septic bursitis): Typically involves superficial bursae (e.g., olecranon or prepatellar), not common in the toe; no fever or risk factors mentioned.
- D (Degenerative joint disease): Osteoarthritis is chronic and degenerative, not acute onset.
- E (Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease): Can present acutely (pseudogout) but often affects knees or wrists, not typically the first MTP joint; uric acid is elevated, not calcium-related.
- F (Rheumatoid arthritis): Chronic symmetric polyarthritis, not acute monoarthritis.

The presentation with podagra, hyperuricemia, and diuretic use strongly points to gouty arthritis.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 47-year-old man comes to the office for a routine examination before beginning an exercise program. He feels well. He has a 
25-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus. A grade 1/6, early peaking, systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the left third 
intercostal space. Sensation to light touch is mildly decreased over the toes. The remainder of the examination shows no 
abnormalities. An exercise stress test shows a 3-mm ST-segment depression early in the test. The test does not produce any pain. 
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
The patient has type 1 diabetes for 25 years, which puts him at risk for both microvascular and macrovascular complications.  
The murmur described (grade 1/6, early peaking, systolic ejection murmur at left third intercostal space) is most consistent with a flow murmur or possibly mild aortic sclerosis rather than severe aortic stenosis.  
The key finding is the **3-mm ST-segment depression early in the exercise stress test without pain** — this indicates **silent myocardial ischemia**, which is common in diabetic patients with autonomic neuropathy.  
The mild decreased sensation in the toes suggests diabetic neuropathy, supporting the possibility of autonomic neuropathy.  
The most likely underlying cause of the ischemia is **atherosclerotic heart disease**, given his long-standing diabetes and risk factors.  

<answer>C</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 22-year-old man comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. He feels well and has not noticed any health issues. 
He is in the US Army. Two months ago, he returned from a 10-month deployment to Southwest Asia, during which he started 
smoking one pack of cigarettes daily. The patient says he is happy to be home and has been celebrating with his friends and family. He has been consuming an average of six beers daily since returning to the United States. He has no history of major 
medical illness. He does not use any medications or other substances. His mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. The patient is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m
2. His first blood pressure reading is 156/95 mm Hg; repeat reading 
is 160/92 mm Hg. He says he remembers his blood pressure being normal when he had it checked in the past. Physical 
examination discloses no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's increased blood 
pressure?

options.D: Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla
options.E: Atherosclerotic narrowing of the renal artery

results.response:
The patient has newly elevated blood pressure with a likely normal prior baseline.  
He has two major recent changes: heavy smoking (1 pack/day) and heavy alcohol intake (6 beers/day).  
While both can raise BP, acute alcohol consumption in large amounts is a well-known cause of reversible hypertension, and his pattern of "celebrating" since returning home (2 months of heavy drinking) fits a temporal relationship.  
Tobacco use tends to cause acute transient spikes, but sustained hypertension from smoking alone is less likely than from heavy daily alcohol in this context.  
Primary hypertension is possible but less likely given age, normal BMI, and clear recent behavioral change.  
Pheochromocytoma and renal artery atherosclerosis are much less likely without suggestive symptoms or risk factors.  

The most likely cause here is high levels of alcohol consumption.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 32-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of a painless rash over his arms. During the past month, he 
also has had a 3.2-kg (7-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. 
He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (158 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Pulse is 98/min; other vital signs are within normal 
limits. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. A photograph of the left upper extremity is 
shown. Similar findings are noted over the right upper extremity. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's 
dermatologic findings?
results.response:
The rash is described as painless and on the arms, with an accompanying unintentional weight loss. The key clue is the association of the rash with weight loss despite no change in appetite, which suggests a possible underlying systemic condition, such as a malignancy or autoimmune disorder.  

The options include:  

- **A. Sun-induced dermal keratinocyte dysplasia** — This would be actinic keratosis, which is usually related to sun exposure and not typically linked to weight loss.  
- **B. Celiac disease-associated skin condition** — Dermatitis herpetiformis is intensely itchy, not painless, and weight loss in celiac is usually due to malabsorption (reduced appetite or GI symptoms).  
- **C. Kaposi sarcoma** — Usually presents as violaceous plaques/nodules, can be associated with weight loss if HIV/AIDS or other immunosuppression is present, but no risk factors mentioned.  
- **D. Lichen planus** — Pruritic, violaceous, flat-topped papules; not typically linked to weight loss.  
- **E. Seborrheic dermatitis** — Presents as scaly, erythematous patches in sebaceous areas (scalp, face), not linked to weight loss.  

The presentation of a painless rash with weight loss and no other symptoms could point toward **dermatitis herpetiformis (celiac disease)** if the rash is pruritic, but the stem says "painless," which is atypical. Alternatively, **necrolytic migratory erythema** (glucagonoma syndrome) could cause a rash with weight loss, but that’s not listed.  

Given the listed options, the only one that could be linked to both a rash and unexplained weight loss is **B. Celiac disease-associated skin condition** (dermatitis herpetiformis can sometimes be mild in itching or described as painless by some patients, and weight loss can occur in celiac even without diarrhea). The other options have no strong tie to weight loss.  

However, I’m not fully confident because the rash isn’t described in detail (no photo) and “painless” is unusual for dermatitis herpetiformis. But among the choices, B is the most plausible systemic link.  

<answer>B</answer>  
<belief>0.65</belief>

results.question:
A 42-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of hot flashes and intermittent palpitations. 
Her last menstrual period was 6 months ago. Menses previously occurred at regular 29-day intervals but gradually had occurred 
less frequently during the past 2 years. She underwent ovarian cystectomy 13 years ago for a small benign teratoma. She used an 
oral contraceptive for 12 years until tubal ligation 6 years ago. Her mother and sister had menopause at the ages of 47 and 49 
years, respectively. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 26 years. She is sexually active and monogamous 
with one male partner. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (191 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Pulse is 82/min and blood 
pressure is 130/85 mm Hg. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, discloses no abnormalities. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Determination of the serum follicle-stimulating hormone level
options.B: Assessment of LH levels in serum
options.E: Transvaginal ultrasound of the uterus and adnexa

results.correct_answer: Determination of the serum follicle-stimulating hormone level
results.predicted_answer: Determination of the serum follicle-stimulating hormone level
results.response:
The patient has secondary amenorrhea for 6 months with vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, palpitations), consistent with perimenopause/menopause.  
Her age (42) is earlier than her family history (mother at 47, sister at 49), but risk factors include smoking (accelerates ovarian aging) and possibly ovarian surgery history.  
The most appropriate next diagnostic step is to confirm menopause by checking serum follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which would be elevated in menopause.  

LH (B) can also be elevated but FSH is the standard initial test. Testosterone (C) is for hirsutism or virilization. ECG (D) is unrelated to diagnosing menopause. Transvaginal ultrasound (E) is not indicated without abnormal pelvic exam or bleeding.  

<answer>A</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A colleague of a 47-ye ar-old physician who is conducting patient evaluations during afternoon walk-in clinic hours notices that 
the physician appears groggy, has slurred speech, and smells of alcohol. Earlier in the day, the physician had attended a celebratory lunch for the graduation of one of the clinic's employees from nursing school. The colleague has not witnessed similar 
findings in the physician in the past. The physician is scheduled to work at the clinic for the next 4 hours. A physician assistant is present at the clinic and typically aids the physician with evaluating patients. Which of the following is the most appropriate action for the colleague to take at this time?

options.A: Permit the physician to complete the scheduled clinical duties, but express concern regarding the incident at the subsequent physicians' staff meeting.
options.B: Instruct the clinic manager to cancel the remaining scheduled appointments for the day
options.C: Instruct the physician assistant to evaluate and discharge the patients prior to the physician's examination.
options.D: Confront the physician and request that he depart from the clinic immediately
options.E: Inform the clinic administrator of the physician's condition and proceed with the remaining clinic schedule in the physician's absence.
options.F: Notify the state medical licensing authority about the physician's condition

results.correct_answer: Inform the clinic administrator of the physician's condition and proceed with the remaining clinic schedule in the physician's absence.
results.predicted_answer: Inform the clinic administrator of the physician's condition and proceed with the remaining clinic schedule in the physician's absence.

<answer>E</answer>

results.question:
A 68-year-old woman returns to the office for a follow-up examination. One month ago, urinalysis showed protein during work-
up of a suspected urinary tract infection. At that time, the patient was prescribed a 3-day course of oral cephalexin. Today, she 
says her previous pain on urination and urinary frequency have resolved. She has major depressive disorder and osteoarthritis. Current medications are venlafaxine and acetaminophen. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 66/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 122/66 mm Hg. The patient appears physically fit. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Repeat 
urinalysis is shown: 
 
Specific gravity  1.015 (N=1.003 –1.029)  
pH 6.0 (N=4.5 –7.8) 
Protein  negative  
Blood  negative  
Leukocyte esterase  negative  
Nitrite  negative  
WBCs  3/hpf  
RBCs  1/hpf  
 
 Which of the following is the most appropriate additional diagnostic study at this time?

options.A: Measurement of the urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio
options.B: Quantitative assessment of urinary protein excretion over a 24-hour period
options.D: Urine protein detection via sulfosalicylic acid precipitation
options.E: Further diagnostic evaluation is not warranted

results.correct_answer: Further diagnostic evaluation is not warranted
results.predicted_answer: Further diagnostic evaluation is not warranted
results.response:
<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

The patient presented with symptoms of a urinary tract infection one month ago, which resolved after a course of cephalexin. The repeat urinalysis now shows no protein, no blood, no leukocyte esterase, no nitrite, and only minimal WBCs and RBCs. All parameters are within normal limits. Given that the proteinuria was transient and likely related to the resolved infection, and there are no current abnormal findings or symptoms suggesting renal disease, further diagnostic evaluation for proteinuria is not warranted. The patient has no hypertension, edema, or other signs of renal impairment. The medications (venlafaxine and acetaminophen) are not strongly associated with causing proteinuria. Therefore, option E is the most appropriate.

Belief is high (0.95) because the urinalysis is completely normal post-treatment, and clinical guidelines do not recommend further workup for transient proteinuria that has resolved without other indicators of renal disease.

results.question:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of severe right lower leg pain since he fell in the woods 
during a camping trip 2 days ago. The pain has worsened during the past 12 hours. He sustained a small scratch below his knee 
as a result of the fall. He has no history of serious illness and uses no medications or other substances. He does not drink alcohol. 
Pulse is 110/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. Examination of the right lower extremity shows 
edema, diffuse cellulitis, and hemorrhagic bullae from the knee to the ankle. No abnormalities of the left lower extremity are 
noted. Peripheral intravenous antibiotic therapy and infusion of 2 L of crystalloid fluid are begun. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Aspiration of the hemorrhagic bullae using a fine needle
options.C: Obtain magnetic resonance imaging of the affected lower extremity.
options.D: Incision and removal of necrotic tissue
options.E: Hospitalization for continued monitoring without additional intervention

results.correct_answer: Incision and removal of necrotic tissue
results.predicted_answer: Incision and removal of necrotic tissue
results.response:
The patient's presentation (history of trauma, rapidly progressive cellulitis with hemorrhagic bullae, systemic toxicity) is classic for necrotizing fasciitis. The most critical step in management is immediate surgical exploration with debridement of necrotic tissue to control the infection and remove the source of toxins.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
A 27-year-old wo man, gravida 3, para 2, at 12 weeks' gestation comes to the office for her first prenatal visit. Her first child was 
delivered at 41 weeks' gestation and weighed 4167 g (9 lb 3 oz). Her second child was delivered at 37 weeks' gestation and 
weighed 4309 g (9 lb 8 oz). She has no history of serious illness and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Her sister has a 
history of delivering a stillborn infant. Her mother and father both have type 2 diabetes mellitus. Eight months ago, the patient immigrated to the United States from Mexico. The patient is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall. She weighed 95 kg (210 lb) prior to her 
pregnancy; BMI was 38 kg/m
2. She has had a 3-kg (6.6-lb) weight gain during her pregnancy. Pulse is 80/min, respirations are 
14/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Bimanual examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 12-week gestation. Ultrasonography confirms a 12-week gestation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Oral glucose tolerance test following an overnight fast
options.D: Determination of maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein level
options.E: Assessment of serum free T4 level

results.correct_answer: Oral glucose tolerance test following an overnight fast
results.predicted_answer: Oral glucose tolerance test following an overnight fast
results.response:
The patient has risk factors for gestational diabetes: obesity (BMI 38), prior macrosomic infants (>4000 g), family history of type 2 diabetes, and possibly Hispanic ethnicity.  
ACOG recommends screening for gestational diabetes at or before 15 weeks in high-risk patients, so an early oral glucose tolerance test is appropriate.  

<answer>C</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
An 84-year-old woman c omes to the office because of a 1-month history of malaise. She has not had fever, weight loss, or 
abdominal pain. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus well controlled with long-acting insulin and metformin. She has missed multiple 
appointments during the past 6 months with no reasons given. Her hemoglobin A 1c 6 months ago was 6.7%. She says she has 
been “feeling fine.” Her son moved back into her home 9 months ago because he had lost his job. The patient’s husband died 5 
years ago, and she has no other children. She appears unkempt. She is alert and fully oriented. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and 
weighs 77 kg (170 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Her pulse is 80/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 160/94 
mm Hg. Mucous membranes are dry; there is no thrush. The skin is dry and intact. The remainder of the physical examination 
shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows no memory deficit. She has a normal mood and flat affect. Her fasting 
serum glucose concentration is 380 mg/dL. Results of urinalysis are shown: 
 
Glucose  4+ 
Ketones  none  
Nitrites  none  
Leukocyte esteras e none  
 
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
The patient presents with poorly controlled diabetes (fasting glucose 380 mg/dL and hemoglobin A1c likely elevated now despite previous 6.7%), unkempt appearance, missed appointments, dry mucous membranes and skin (suggesting dehydration), and social stressors (son moving back, widowhood). Her flat affect and reported malaise without fever, weight loss, or abdominal pain are notable. The urinalysis shows glucosuria without ketonuria, infection, or other abnormalities, ruling out diabetic ketoacidosis and urinary tract infection. The history suggests she may not be adhering to her insulin regimen or dietary advice due to underlying issues. However, the social context (son moving in, missed appointments, unkempt appearance) and flat affect point to possible neglect or depression. Neglect of an older adult is a concern given her age, dependency, and son's potential role as a caregiver who might be failing to provide adequate care. Depression is common in elderly with chronic illnesses and loss, but she denies memory deficit and has normal mood with flat affect (which can be a depressive symptom). Yet, the neglect seems more prominent as the son may not be assisting with her medical needs, leading to poor diabetes control. Pancreatic carcinoma is unlikely without weight loss, pain, or jaundice. Asymptomatic bacteriuria is ruled out by negative nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Poor adherence is likely but is a consequence rather than the primary diagnosis; neglect or depression may be the root cause. Given the evidence, neglect is the most likely underlying issue, as she may not be receiving necessary support from her son.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question:
A 15-year-old boy is brought to the office 2 hours after he awoke with weakness of the right side of his face. He has a 2-week 
history of intermittent headache, sensitivity to light, and mild to moderate neck pain. He has no history of serious illness. His 
only medication is acetaminophen as needed. He lives in New Jersey and has not traveled outside of the state during the past 6 
months. Temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse is 78/min and regular, and blood pressure is 108/70 mm Hg. Flexion of the neck 
elicits moderate pain and resistance. The right eyelids do not close completely. Ocular movements are normal. There is no 
evidence of ptosis. The right side of the forehead does not wrinkle when the patient looks up. There is severe weakness of the right corner of the mouth. There are no abnormalities of the left side of the face. Muscle strength in the jaw, tongue, and palate 
is normal and symmetric. There is no dysarthria. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all extremities. Coordination and sensation are intact. Gait is normal. A lumbar puncture is performed, and opening pressure is 180 mm H
2O. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis results are 
shown: 
 
Glucose  60 mg/dL  
Total protein  52 mg/dL  
WBC  85/mm3 
 
 Segmented neutrophils  30% 
Lymphocytes  70% 
RBC  1/mm3 
 
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Obtaining a serum angiotensin-converting enzyme level
options.B: Serologic testing for Lyme disease (Borreli a burgdorferi)
options.C: Cytomegalovirus DNA amplification by polymerase chain reaction

results.correct_answer: Serologic testing for Lyme disease (Borreli a burgdorferi)
results.predicted_answer: Serologic testing for Lyme disease (Borreli a burgdorferi)
results.response:
The patient presents with acute right-sided facial nerve palsy (Bell's palsy-like presentation) preceded by headache, photophobia, and neck pain, with CSF showing lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose.  
This fits Lyme neuroborreliosis (common in New Jersey, tick exposure possible even without travel), which often causes facial palsy and can have CSF findings like these.  

The most appropriate next diagnostic step is serologic testing for Lyme disease.  

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department because of loud breathing, harsh cough, and a hoarse cry since she 
awoke 1 hour ago. Her parents state that yesterday she had a runny nose. Medical history is unremarkable and she receives no 
medications. Temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 32/min, and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. 
There are moderate suprasternal and subcostal retractions. Inspiratory stridor is heard at rest. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.B: Inhaled albuterol administered via a nebulizer

results.response:
The patient is a 2-year-old girl presenting with acute onset of loud breathing, harsh cough, hoarse cry, and inspiratory stridor at rest, along with retractions and a history of runny nose. This clinical picture is classic for croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), which is often viral in origin (e.g., parainfluenza virus). The symptoms are moderate to severe given the presence of stridor at rest and retractions. Initial management of croup focuses on reducing airway inflammation and edema.

Key points:
- Croup is typically managed with glucocorticoids to decrease inflammation, which provides sustained improvement. Dexamethasone is the preferred corticosteroid due to its long duration of action.
- Nebulized epinephrine is used in cases with significant respiratory distress (e.g., stridor at rest, retractions) for immediate relief by causing vasoconstriction and reducing edema, but its effects are short-lived.
- Albuterol (a bronchodilator) is not effective in croup because the pathology involves the upper airway (larynx and trachea) rather than the lower airways (bronchi), so it is not indicated.
- Inhaled budesonide may be used as an alternative to dexamethasone but is not as preferred due to the need for nebulization and less evidence compared to systemic dexamethasone.
- Oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) are effective but dexamethasone is often preferred due to longer half-life and single dose.
- Subcutaneous epinephrine is not standard; nebulized epinephrine is the route for upper airway effects.

For initial pharmacotherapy in this case with moderate to severe symptoms (stridor at rest and retractions), the most appropriate initial step is to administer nebulized epinephrine for immediate relief of airway obstruction. However, glucocorticoids (e.g., dexamethasone) are also essential and should be given concurrently for sustained effect. But the question asks for "initial pharmacotherapy," and in an emergency setting with significant distress, nebulized epinephrine is often the first drug given for rapid effect, followed by steroids.

Reviewing the options:
A. IV dexamethasone – effective but invasive; oral/IM dexamethasone is equally effective and preferred for non-critical cases.
B. Inhaled albuterol – not effective for croup.
C. Inhaled budesonide – an option but not first-line; dexamethasone is preferred.
D. Nebulized epinephrine – indicated for moderate to severe croup with stridor at rest for rapid improvement.
E. Oral albuterol – not effective.
F. Oral prednisone – effective but dexamethasone is preferred.
G. Subcutaneous epinephrine – not standard; nebulized is route of choice.

Given the acuity with stridor at rest and retractions, nebulized epinephrine (D) is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy to quickly reduce airway obstruction. This is consistent with guidelines (e.g., AAP) which recommend nebulized epinephrine for severe symptoms or stridor at rest. Steroids should be given promptly as well, but epinephrine acts faster for immediate relief.

Thus, the correct answer is D.
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question:
A 28-year-old man is admitted to the intensive care unit 3 hours after undergoing resection and anastomosis of a small-bowel 
injury and nephrectomy because of a right renal laceration sustained in a motor vehicle collision. He also has bilateral, minimally displaced pubic rami fractures. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. He is receiving morphine and propofol. He responds 
only to painful stimuli. Pulse is 110/min, ventilatory rate is 12/min, and blood pressure is 133/68 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and soft, and the incision is clean, dry, and intact. Since the operation, urine output had been 40 mL/h and bloody. 
During the past hour, urine output has been 5 mL and bloody. In addition to intravenous administration of 0.9% saline, which of 
the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Obtaining computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis
options.C: Administration of a transfusion of packed red blood cells

results.response:
The patient has a recent trauma with small bowel resection, nephrectomy, and pelvic fractures.  
Urine output dropped suddenly from 40 mL/h to 5 mL/h despite adequate blood pressure.  
The urine is bloody, suggesting possible injury or obstruction in the urinary tract.  
Given the pelvic fractures and trauma, urethral injury or bladder injury could cause obstruction.  
Bladder ultrasonography (option D) is a quick, noninvasive way to check for bladder distension from possible urethral disruption or clots.  
CT (A) is less appropriate first step given the acute drop in urine output and need for rapid diagnosis.  
Exploratory laparotomy (B) is premature without first checking for urinary obstruction.  
Transfusion (C) is not indicated based on the information given (no signs of ongoing hemorrhage or low Hgb).  

Most appropriate: bladder ultrasonography to rule out obstruction.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to the office because of a 3-day history of  diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and vomiting. She has 
cerebral palsy, situs inversus, and severe scoliosis. She uses a wheelchair for ambulation. During previous visits, transferring this patient from her wheelchair to the examination table has been time-consuming and has caused the patient discomfort. Today, the patient appears uncomfortable. The physician decides to conduct the examination while the patient is sitting in her wheelchair. 
Temperature is 38.3°C (101.0°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 100%. Examination shows mildly dry mucous membranes. The physician evaluated four previous patients today who had vomiting and fever; he diagnosed gastroenteritis in all four patients. The physician attributes 
this patient's increased pulse to mild dehydration and fever, and he diagnoses this patient with gastroenteritis. The physician prescribes ondansetron and tells the patient to return if she has intractable vomiting or if her abdominal pain worsens. Five hours 
later, the patient is taken to the emergency department after her mother finds her listless and barely responsive. Temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F), pulse is 180/min, respirations are 35/min, and blood pressure is 70/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96%. CT scan of the abdomen shows volvulus, and emergency resection of necrotic bowel is done. 
Which of the following best describes the error that occurred?

options.D: Failure to consider alternative diagnoses after reaching an initial conclusion

results.correct_answer: Failure to consider alternative diagnoses after reaching an initial conclusion
results.predicted_answer: Failure to consider alternative diagnoses after reaching an initial conclusion
results.response:
The patient had situs inversus, which can predispose to midgut volvulus due to malrotation. The physician anchored on the diagnosis of gastroenteritis (like the previous four patients) despite the patient’s unique anatomy and did not reconsider when findings (tachycardia, abdominal pain, fever) could be explained by a more serious condition. This is an example of **anchoring bias** — failing to adjust the initial diagnosis after receiving initial clues.  

That matches: **Failure to consider alternative diagnoses after reaching an initial conclusion**.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 27-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-month history  of depressed mood and fatigue. He reports that it takes him 
at least 1 hour to fall asleep at night. He has had decreased concentration at work and decreased interest in socializing with his 
friends. He no longer exercises. He has epilepsy well controlled with levetiracetam. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a sad mood and is briefly tearful. He has no suicidal ideation, hallucinations, 
or delusions. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
results.response:
The patient has symptoms of major depressive disorder (insomnia, depressed mood, fatigue, anhedonia, poor concentration).  
He is on levetiracetam for epilepsy, which can sometimes cause or worsen depressive symptoms.  
The key point is that he has significant sleep-onset insomnia (takes 1 hour to fall asleep).  

Among the options:  
- **Alprazolam** (A) is a benzodiazepine; not first-line for depression, risk of dependence, and may affect seizure threshold.  
- **Aripiprazole** (B) is an antipsychotic, usually an adjunct for treatment-resistant depression, not first-line monotherapy here.  
- **Bupropion** (C) can worsen anxiety and insomnia, and lowers seizure threshold — risky with epilepsy history despite being controlled.  
- **Buspirone** (D) is for anxiety, not major depression as first-line.  
- **Mirtazapine** (E) is sedating, helps with sleep onset, safe in epilepsy, and good for depression with insomnia and poor appetite.  

Mirtazapine is the most appropriate given his presentation and medication profile.  

<answer>E</answer>  
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 23-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office because she has been unable to conceive during the past year. She and her 
husband have had regular unprotected intercourse during this time. The husband has no children. Prior to attempting to conceive, 
the patient regularly used depot medroxyprogesterone. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals with occasional mid-cycle 
abdominal pain previously diagnosed as mittelschmerz. She had a ruptured appendix at age 17 years and pelvic inflammatory 
disease at age 19 years. At age 21 years, cervical cytology showed a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion; repeat cervical 
cytology 1 year later showed no abnormalities. Current medications are a prenatal vitamin and occasional ibuprofen for mid-
cycle abdominal pain. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal 
limits. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Hysterosalpingography is done, and x-rays obtained during the procedure 
are shown. Without treatment, which of the following is the most likely clinical course for this patient?

options.A: Infertility attributable to a cause in the woman
options.D: Spontaneous conception and delivery within 12 months

results.correct_answer: Infertility attributable to a cause in the woman
results.predicted_answer: Infertility attributable to a cause in the woman
results.response:
The hysterosalpingogram likely shows tubal pathology (hydrosalpinx or blockage) given her history of ruptured appendix and pelvic inflammatory disease, which are risk factors for tubal factor infertility.  
Since the question says “without treatment,” the most likely course is continued infertility due to a female factor (tubal blockage).  

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 50-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She feels well. Medical history is unremarkable 
and she takes no medications. There is no family history of serious illness. Physical examination, including breast examination, discloses no abnormalities. Mammography shows a cluster of 20 pleomorphic microcalcifications in a 1-cm area at the 2-o'clock 
position of the right breast. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: A cluster of pleomorphic microcalcifications is most consistent with ductal carcinoma in situ.

results.correct_answer: A cluster of pleomorphic microcalcifications is most consistent with ductal carcinoma in situ.
results.predicted_answer: A cluster of pleomorphic microcalcifications is most consistent with ductal carcinoma in situ.
results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A community has created standard-of-care guidelines for ambulatory patients with several common diagnoses. The community's 
standard of care for patients with asthma is annual pulmonary function testing. Recent analysis of billing records from one clinic 
in this community shows that only 34% of patients with asthma at the clinic undergo pulmonary function testing. Enacting which 
of the following procedures is most likely to improve this clinic's adherence to the community's standard of care for patients with 
asthma?

options.A: Annual audits of asthma patient records with performance reports provided to clinicians
options.B: Automated prompts linked to the asthma diagnosis in the electronic health record
options.C: Posting notices in the waiting area that prompt asthmatic patients to obtain yearly pulmonary function tests
options.D: Performing pulmonary function testing on every patient evaluated for respiratory complaints
options.E: Performing pulmonary function testing on asthma patients during every visit that follows an exacerbation

results.correct_answer: Automated prompts linked to the asthma diagnosis in the electronic health record
results.predicted_answer: Automated prompts linked to the asthma diagnosis in the electronic health record
results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.9</belief>

results.question:
A 27-y ear-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of moderate pain in her 
right breast. Three weeks ago, she underwent cesarean delivery of a healthy newborn at term because of breech presentation. She 
is breast-feeding, but the newborn is not latching properly on the right. She has no history of serious illness. Medications are 
acetaminophen for postoperative pain and a prenatal vitamin. Temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F). Examination of the right breast 
shows a 5 × 2-cm wedge-shaped area of erythema and moderate tenderness. The left breast is normal. Abdominal examination shows a well-healing surgical incision. In addition to beginning antipyretic therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate 
next step in management?

options.A: Tightly wrapping the right breast with a constrictive bandage
options.D: Aspiration of the erythematous region using a fine-bore needle

results.response:
The patient has mastitis, which is common in the postpartum period, especially with breastfeeding difficulties (poor latching). The presentation of fever, localized wedge-shaped erythema, tenderness, and pain is classic for bacterial mastitis, most often caused by *Staphylococcus aureus*. The most appropriate next step is antibiotic therapy targeting this organism, and dicloxacillin is a first-line treatment.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question:
A 12-year-old girl is brought to the office for a well-child examination. She feels well. She has no history of serious illness and 
receives no medications. Menarche has not yet occurred. She is at the 30th percentile for height and 60th percentile for weight. 
Examination of the breasts shows no glandular tissue; the areolae follow the skin contours of the chest. There is no pubic hair. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Assess serum levels of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone
options.B: Determination of serum growth hormone and thyroxine levels
options.D: Obtaining a radiograph of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment
options.E: Reassurance and routine follow-up are indicated.

results.correct_answer: Reassurance and routine follow-up are indicated.
results.predicted_answer: Obtaining a radiograph of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment
results.response:
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.9</belief>

results.question:
A 42-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-month history of episodes of light-headedness and loss of consciousness 
for 10 seconds. The episodes occur two to three times weekly at work. She says her light-headedness resolves after she eats 
snacks. During this time, she also has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no 
medications. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. 
Physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. Serum glucose concentration is 41 mg/dL, serum C peptide concentration is 0.5 ng/mL (N=0.5–2.5), and serum insulin concentration is 80 μIU/mL (N=5–20). CT scan of 
the abdomen is most likely to show which of the following findings?

options.A: A mass in the duodenum accompanied by metastatic lesions in the liver
options.B: Cystic lesion located in the space posterior to the stomach and anterior to the pancreas
options.C: Hypodense lesion in the pancreatic head causing main pancreatic duct obstruction
options.D: Pancreatic neck lesion with vascular characteristics

results.response:
Let’s break this down.  

The patient has:  
- Recurrent hypoglycemia with neuroglycopenic symptoms (light-headedness, LOC) relieved by eating.
